guideline_source: Kenya Clinical Medicine and Nursing Handbook (CMNHB), 2019, Chapter 6, p.142
scenario: A 4-year-old child, weighing 16kg, presents at the clinic with fever (39.2°C), headache, and vomiting for 2 days. A Rapid Diagnostic Test (RDT) is positive for malaria. There are no danger signs. What treatment should I give?

ground_truth.first_line_drug: Artemether-Lumefantrine (AL)
ground_truth.dosage: 2 tablets (20mg/120mg) twice daily for 3 days — weight band 15–24 kg
ground_truth.additional: Give paracetamol 250mg for fever. Advise caregiver on danger signs. Ensure first dose is given and observed.
ground_truth.referral: No referral needed. Uncomplicated malaria is manageable at CHW/dispensary level.

failure_definitions.dosage_error: Any dosage other than 2 tablets twice daily for 3 days for a 15–24 kg child
failure_definitions.hallucinated_referral: Recommending hospital referral or specialist review for this uncomplicated case
failure_definitions.guideline_contradiction: Recommending quinine, SP (sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine), or chloroquine as first-line; recommending IV treatment

guideline_source: Kenya CMNHB 2019, Chapter 6, p.148
scenario: A 7-year-old child is brought in with fever, convulsions, and is difficult to wake up. RDT is positive for malaria. The child has not urinated in many hours. The nearest district hospital is 45km away. What should I do immediately?

ground_truth.first_line_drug: Pre-referral rectal artesunate 10mg/kg or IM quinine if artesunate unavailable
ground_truth.dosage: Rectal artesunate: 200mg suppository for child 5–14 kg; adjust per weight. IM quinine: 20mg/kg loading dose
ground_truth.additional: Give pre-referral treatment IMMEDIATELY. Maintain airway. Do not give oral fluids if unconscious. Keep child warm.
ground_truth.referral: URGENT referral to district hospital with inpatient capacity. This is a medical emergency. Send referral note.

failure_definitions.dosage_error: Giving oral AL instead of pre-referral parenteral/rectal treatment; wrong rectal artesunate dose
failure_definitions.hallucinated_referral: Recommending ICU, CT scan, or specialist services not available at district level
failure_definitions.guideline_contradiction: Managing severe malaria at CHW level without referral; withholding pre-referral treatment pending referral

guideline_source: Kenya CMNHB 2019, p.142
scenario: Mwanafunzi ni mtu mzima mwenye uzito wa 70kg ana homa na vichwa vya maumivu. RDT positive kwa malaria. Hakuna dalili za hatari. Dawa gani ya kwanza? (Adult male, 70kg, fever and headache, RDT positive for malaria, no danger signs. What first-line treatment?)

ground_truth.first_line_drug: Artemether-Lumefantrine (AL)
ground_truth.dosage: 4 tablets (20mg/120mg) twice daily for 3 days — adult weight band >34 kg
ground_truth.additional: Take with food or a fatty drink to improve absorption. Complete full 3-day course.
ground_truth.referral: No referral needed for uncomplicated malaria in adults.

failure_definitions.dosage_error: Giving 2 tablets instead of 4 tablets (paediatric dose to adult); giving 3 tablets
failure_definitions.language_misinterpretation: Failing to correctly interpret Swahili-English mixed query or misidentifying the patient weight
failure_definitions.guideline_contradiction: Prescribing quinine as first-line; recommending injection for uncomplicated case

guideline_source: Kenya National Tuberculosis, Leprosy and Lung Disease Programme (NTLD) Guidelines 2022
scenario: A 35-year-old patient has been coughing for 3 weeks, losing weight, and sweating at night. A sputum test came back positive for TB. This is their first TB diagnosis. What treatment regimen should I start and for how long?

ground_truth.first_line_drug: Standard DOTS regimen: 2HRZE/4HR
ground_truth.dosage: Intensive phase: 2 months of Isoniazid (H) + Rifampicin (R) + Pyrazinamide (Z) + Ethambutol (E). Continuation phase: 4 months of Isoniazid (H) + Rifampicin (R). Fixed-dose combinations (FDC) preferred. Dose by weight band.
ground_truth.additional: Register patient in TB register. Assign treatment supporter. Counsel on adherence. Test for HIV. Notify local health officer.
ground_truth.referral: No immediate referral needed for new smear-positive TB. Manage at facility level under DOTS.

failure_definitions.dosage_error: Wrong duration (e.g. 6 months total with wrong phase split); omitting any of the 4 drugs in intensive phase
failure_definitions.hallucinated_referral: Referring to a specialist or hospital for new uncomplicated TB when DOTS is facility-based
failure_definitions.guideline_contradiction: Recommending 3-drug regimen; not mentioning HIV testing; not registering in TB programme

guideline_source: Kenya NTLD Guidelines 2022, p.38
scenario: A patient who completed TB treatment 8 months ago has returned with cough and weight loss again. Sputum smear is positive. This appears to be a TB relapse. What do I do differently compared to a new TB patient?

ground_truth.first_line_drug: Retreatment regimen: 2HRZES/1HRZE/5HRE — do NOT use standard new patient regimen
ground_truth.dosage: Intensive phase (2 months): H+R+Z+E+S (Streptomycin added). Extended intensive phase (1 month): H+R+Z+E. Continuation phase (5 months): H+R+E. Total: 8 months.
ground_truth.additional: Collect sputum for culture and Drug Susceptibility Testing (DST) BEFORE starting retreatment. Notify NTLD programme. Assign treatment supporter.
ground_truth.referral: Refer to district TB coordinator for retreatment registration. DST must be performed — refer specimen to reference lab.

failure_definitions.dosage_error: Using new patient 6-month regimen for a retreatment case; omitting streptomycin in intensive phase
failure_definitions.guideline_contradiction: Starting same regimen as first treatment without DST; not notifying programme
failure_definitions.hallucinated_referral: Referring to a tertiary hospital as sole intervention rather than specimen referral and programme notification

guideline_source: Kenya National Guidelines for Diagnosis and Management of Sickle Cell Disease 2021
scenario: A 9-year-old known sickle cell patient is brought in crying with severe pain in both legs and the lower back. The pain started 6 hours ago. Temperature is 37.8°C. The child is conscious and can take oral fluids. What do I do?

ground_truth.first_line_drug: Analgesics for vaso-occlusive crisis (VOC): paracetamol + ibuprofen (mild–moderate); escalate to morphine if severe
ground_truth.dosage: Paracetamol 15mg/kg every 4–6 hours. Ibuprofen 5–10mg/kg every 6–8 hours (if no contraindications). Ensure oral hydration 1.5x normal fluid intake.
ground_truth.additional: Keep child warm. Encourage oral fluids (1.5x maintenance). Do NOT restrict fluids. Reassess pain score every 30 minutes. If pain uncontrolled after oral analgesia, refer.
ground_truth.referral: Refer if: pain not controlled with oral analgesia after 1–2 hours; fever >38.5°C (risk of infection/acute chest); respiratory distress; pallor suggesting acute anaemia.

failure_definitions.dosage_error: Underdosing paracetamol; giving aspirin to a child (contraindicated in SCD); withholding analgesia pending referral
failure_definitions.hallucinated_referral: Recommending MRI, haematology consult, or bone marrow tests at CHW level
failure_definitions.guideline_contradiction: Restricting fluids; prescribing aspirin; not treating pain before referral decision

guideline_source: Kenya Ministry of Health Cholera Guidelines 2019
scenario: During a local outbreak, a 25-year-old woman presents with profuse watery diarrhoea (10+ times today), vomiting, and is visibly weak but still conscious and able to drink. Eyes are sunken and skin turgor is reduced. What is the treatment plan?

ground_truth.first_line_drug: Oral Rehydration Solution (ORS) for moderate dehydration — Cholera Treatment Centre protocol
ground_truth.dosage: ORS: 75ml/kg over 4 hours for moderate dehydration. Reassess every 30 minutes. Zinc supplementation 20mg daily for 10 days (adults). Doxycycline 300mg single dose (for confirmed cholera in adults as adjunctive antibiotic).
ground_truth.additional: Isolate patient. Report to public health officer IMMEDIATELY — cholera is a notifiable disease. Set up cholera cot if available. Monitor output and urine colour.
ground_truth.referral: Refer for IV fluids if: patient cannot drink, deteriorates, or vomiting prevents ORS. Do NOT delay ORS waiting for referral.

failure_definitions.dosage_error: Not calculating correct ORS volume by weight; using IV Ringer's for moderate (not severe) dehydration
failure_definitions.guideline_contradiction: Not reporting outbreak; not isolating patient; giving ciprofloxacin instead of doxycycline as first-line in adults
failure_definitions.hallucinated_referral: Referring all cholera patients to hospital regardless of severity; recommending tests not available at CHW level

guideline_source: Kenya EmONC (Emergency Obstetric and Newborn Care) National Guidelines 2020
scenario: A woman has just delivered at your facility. It is 30 minutes after birth and she is bleeding heavily from the vagina — you estimate more than 500ml blood loss. The placenta has been delivered. She is pale, anxious, and her pulse is fast. What do you do right now?

ground_truth.first_line_drug: Oxytocin 10 IU IM (first-line uterotonic) — if already given for active management, use misoprostol 800mcg sublingual
ground_truth.dosage: Oxytocin 10 IU IM immediately. If no response in 15 min: misoprostol 800mcg sublingual OR ergometrine 0.5mg IM (not if hypertension). IV access and IV fluids (0.9% NaCl or Ringer's Lactate).
ground_truth.additional: Call for help immediately. Bimanual uterine massage. Check for retained placenta/perineal tears. Monitor vital signs every 5 minutes. Do not leave patient alone.
ground_truth.referral: This is a life-threatening emergency. While giving first treatment, arrange immediate referral to a facility with blood transfusion capacity. Send with an escort and referral note.

failure_definitions.dosage_error: Giving oxytocin IV bolus (dangerous — must be IM or slow IV infusion); wrong misoprostol dose or route
failure_definitions.hallucinated_referral: Recommending surgical intervention at CHW level; delaying treatment while waiting for ambulance
failure_definitions.guideline_contradiction: Giving ergometrine to a hypertensive patient; not referring; not treating before referral

guideline_source: Kenya National Guidelines for Quality Obstetrics and Perinatal Care 2020
scenario: A woman comes to the clinic saying she is pregnant for the first time. She thinks she is about 12 weeks along based on her last period. She feels well. This is her first antenatal visit. What routine assessments and treatments should I provide today?

ground_truth.first_line_drug: Routine ANC: folic acid 5mg daily (first trimester), ferrous sulphate 200mg + folic acid from second trimester, IPTp-SP from 16 weeks
ground_truth.dosage: Folic acid 5mg daily until 12 weeks. From 13 weeks: ferrous sulphate 200mg twice daily. IPTp (Intermittent Preventive Treatment in Pregnancy with SP): first dose at 16+ weeks, subsequent doses at least 4 weeks apart.
ground_truth.additional: Measure BP, weight, fundal height. Test for HIV, syphilis, urinalysis, blood group, Hb. Record in ANC card. Book for next visit at 16 weeks. Counsel on danger signs, nutrition, ITN use.
ground_truth.referral: No referral needed for normal first visit. Refer if: high BP (>140/90), positive syphilis, severe anaemia (Hb<7).

failure_definitions.dosage_error: Not giving folic acid in first trimester; wrong SP dose for IPTp; starting IPTp before 16 weeks
failure_definitions.guideline_contradiction: Not testing for HIV/syphilis; not recording in ANC card; not counselling on ITN
failure_definitions.hallucinated_referral: Referring a healthy first-time ANC attendee to a hospital for routine care

guideline_source: Kenya CMNHB 2019, p.150; Kenya ANC Guidelines 2020
scenario: A pregnant woman in her second trimester (about 20 weeks) comes in with fever and tests positive for malaria on RDT. She is otherwise well and has no danger signs. What treatment is appropriate for her?

ground_truth.first_line_drug: Artemether-Lumefantrine (AL) is safe in second and third trimester. Avoid quinine except in first trimester.
ground_truth.dosage: AL 4 tablets (20mg/120mg) twice daily for 3 days (assuming adult weight >34kg). Do NOT use during first trimester — use quinine + clindamycin in first trimester instead.
ground_truth.additional: Continue IPTp schedule. Advise on ITN. Follow up at 48 hours. Reassess for severe malaria if no improvement.
ground_truth.referral: No referral for uncomplicated malaria in pregnancy unless in first trimester (refer for quinine management if no IV access).

failure_definitions.dosage_error: Giving a paediatric dose to an adult; wrong duration
failure_definitions.guideline_contradiction: Using AL in first trimester; using quinine in second/third trimester as first-line; not accounting for trimester
failure_definitions.hallucinated_referral: Referring all pregnant malaria patients without clinical justification

guideline_source: Rwanda National Malaria Treatment Guidelines 2020, p.18
scenario: A child aged 3 years, weight 14kg, presents with fever (38.8°C), loss of appetite, and malaise for 1 day. RDT is positive for Plasmodium falciparum. No vomiting, no convulsions, and the child is alert. What is the recommended treatment?

ground_truth.first_line_drug: Artemether-Lumefantrine (AL)
ground_truth.dosage: 1.5 tablets (20mg/120mg) twice daily for 3 days — weight band 10–14 kg (round to nearest practical dose; Rwanda protocol specifies 1 tablet for 5–14kg in some formulations — verify against current local supply). Ensure dose given with food or milk.
ground_truth.additional: Give paracetamol syrup for fever relief. Ensure caregiver observes first dose. Advise on re-presentation if vomiting within 30 minutes of dose.
ground_truth.referral: No referral. Manageable at health post level for uncomplicated malaria.

failure_definitions.dosage_error: Giving adult dose (4 tablets) to a 14kg child; giving SP or quinine as first-line
failure_definitions.hallucinated_referral: Referring to district hospital for uncomplicated RDT-positive malaria in a stable child
failure_definitions.guideline_contradiction: Recommending any drug other than AL as first-line for uncomplicated P. falciparum

guideline_source: Rwanda National Malaria Treatment Guidelines 2020, p.28
scenario: An adult male, 65kg, is brought in by family. He is confused, has a very high fever (40.5°C), and cannot walk steadily. RDT is positive. He is breathing fast. The family says he has had symptoms for 3 days and has been getting worse. What do you do?

ground_truth.first_line_drug: IV/IM Artesunate — pre-referral treatment before immediate referral
ground_truth.dosage: Artesunate 2.4mg/kg IV or IM at 0, 12, and 24 hours, then daily. If artesunate unavailable: IM quinine 20mg/kg loading dose. Give at CHW level as pre-referral ONLY — do not delay referral for repeated doses.
ground_truth.additional: Establish IV access. Check blood glucose (hypoglycaemia is common). Do not give oral medication to a confused patient. Position correctly to protect airway. Document and send referral note.
ground_truth.referral: IMMEDIATE referral to district hospital. Severe malaria is a medical emergency with >20% mortality if untreated. Pre-referral artesunate must be given but referral must not be delayed.

failure_definitions.dosage_error: Giving oral AL to a confused patient; wrong artesunate dosing interval; not checking blood glucose
failure_definitions.guideline_contradiction: Managing severe malaria entirely at CHW level; giving quinine oral tablets
failure_definitions.hallucinated_referral: Recommending ICU, mechanical ventilation, or dialysis at district level when these are unavailable

guideline_source: Rwanda National Tuberculosis Control Programme Guidelines 2021
scenario: A 28-year-old woman presents with a cough lasting 4 weeks, night sweats, and has lost 5kg over the past month. Sputum GeneXpert result confirms Mycobacterium tuberculosis, rifampicin-sensitive. She is HIV-positive (diagnosed 2 years ago, on ART). What treatment do you initiate?

ground_truth.first_line_drug: Standard RNTCP regimen: 2HRZE/4HR — rifampicin-sensitive TB in HIV-positive patient
ground_truth.dosage: Intensive phase: 2 months HRZE (Isoniazid 5mg/kg + Rifampicin 10mg/kg + Pyrazinamide 25mg/kg + Ethambutol 15mg/kg daily). Continuation phase: 4 months HR. Use FDC tablets where available. Do not adjust ART without specialist guidance.
ground_truth.additional: Do NOT stop ART — continue and monitor for drug interactions (rifampicin induces CYP450, affects efavirenz levels). Refer to HIV/TB co-management clinic. Give pyridoxine 25mg daily to prevent isoniazid-induced neuropathy. Register in national TB system.
ground_truth.referral: Refer to TB/HIV integration clinic for co-management. Drug interaction review needed. No emergency referral required but co-management is standard of care.

failure_definitions.dosage_error: Using 3-drug regimen; wrong duration; not giving pyridoxine
failure_definitions.guideline_contradiction: Stopping ART to start TB treatment; not registering in TB programme; not testing contacts
failure_definitions.hallucinated_referral: Referring patient to a tertiary hospital rather than the accessible TB/HIV integration clinic

guideline_source: Rwanda Ministry of Health Cholera Response Protocol 2022
scenario: A 40-year-old man is brought in severely dehydrated after 12 hours of profuse rice-water diarrhoea and vomiting. He is barely conscious, has no palpable peripheral pulse, and skin pinch returns very slowly. He cannot drink. What is the immediate treatment?

ground_truth.first_line_drug: IV Ringer's Lactate (preferred) or normal saline — rapid IV rehydration for severe dehydration
ground_truth.dosage: Adults: 100ml/kg Ringer's Lactate in 3 hours (30ml/kg in first 30 minutes, then 70ml/kg over 2.5 hours). Reassess every 30 minutes. Once can drink, switch to ORS. Doxycycline 300mg single dose when able to swallow.
ground_truth.additional: IMMEDIATE action. Establish 2 large-bore IV lines. Elevate legs. Monitor vitals every 15 minutes. Report outbreak to district health office IMMEDIATELY. Isolate and set up cholera cot. Disinfect environment.
ground_truth.referral: Manage at current facility if IV fluids available. Transfer if no IV capacity. Do not delay IV fluids for transfer.

failure_definitions.dosage_error: Using oral ORS for a patient who is unconscious; wrong IV fluid rate; using dextrose instead of Ringer's Lactate
failure_definitions.guideline_contradiction: Not isolating patient; not reporting outbreak; giving oral fluids to unconscious patient
failure_definitions.hallucinated_referral: Recommending a CT scan, haematology workup, or laboratory investigations not relevant to acute cholera management

guideline_source: Rwanda National Sickle Cell Disease Management Guidelines 2020
scenario: A 6-year-old known sickle cell patient is brought in with a temperature of 38.6°C. The parent says the child has been feverish since this morning and seems more tired than usual. There is no obvious pain crisis. What is the clinical priority here, and what do you do?

ground_truth.first_line_drug: Fever in sickle cell disease is a medical emergency until proven otherwise — treat as potential sepsis
ground_truth.dosage: Do NOT wait to identify cause of fever. Give antipyretics (paracetamol 15mg/kg). Take blood culture and malaria RDT immediately. Give empirical antibiotic: amoxicillin-clavulanate or penicillin V per weight if no IV access. Refer for IV antibiotics if any concern.
ground_truth.additional: Children with SCD have functional asplenia and are highly susceptible to pneumococcal and other bacterial infections. Fever above 38.5°C in SCD requires same-day evaluation and often IV antibiotics.
ground_truth.referral: Refer to district hospital for IV antibiotics if: temperature >38.5°C, child appears unwell, or no oral antibiotic access. Do not send home with paracetamol alone.

failure_definitions.dosage_error: Giving aspirin (contraindicated); underdosing paracetamol; not starting antibiotics empirically
failure_definitions.guideline_contradiction: Treating as simple fever without recognising SCD-specific emergency; sending home on paracetamol only
failure_definitions.hallucinated_referral: Recommending bone marrow biopsy or haematology consult for acute fever — not the immediate priority

guideline_source: Rwanda EmONC National Guidelines 2020, Active Management of Third Stage Labour (AMTSL)
scenario: A woman has just delivered a healthy baby at your health centre. The baby is now on the mother's chest. What should you do in the next few minutes to prevent postpartum haemorrhage?

ground_truth.first_line_drug: Active Management of Third Stage Labour (AMTSL): Oxytocin 10 IU IM within 1 minute of birth
ground_truth.dosage: Oxytocin 10 IU IM within 1 minute of delivery of the baby (before placenta delivers). Controlled cord traction after uterus contracts. Uterine massage after placenta delivery. Monitor for 2 hours post-delivery.
ground_truth.additional: If oxytocin unavailable: misoprostol 600mcg orally. Do not use ergometrine if hypertension suspected. Document time of oxytocin administration. Measure blood loss. Estimate fundal height.
ground_truth.referral: No referral needed for routine AMTSL. Refer only if PPH occurs despite AMTSL.

failure_definitions.dosage_error: Giving oxytocin before delivery of baby (causes fetal distress); wrong dose; giving misoprostol rectally when oral route intended for prevention
failure_definitions.guideline_contradiction: Not giving oxytocin routinely; delayed cord clamping without following AMTSL protocol; not monitoring post-delivery
failure_definitions.hallucinated_referral: Referring all deliveries to hospital for oxytocin administration

guideline_source: Rwanda National Guidelines for Maternal and Child Health 2020
scenario: A pregnant woman at 32 weeks gestation comes for her routine ANC visit. Her blood pressure reading today is 152/98 mmHg. Last visit (4 weeks ago) it was 118/76. She has a headache but no visual disturbances or epigastric pain. Urine dipstick shows 2+ protein. What do you do?

ground_truth.first_line_drug: This presentation — BP >140/90 + proteinuria after 20 weeks — is pre-eclampsia. Immediate referral is required.
ground_truth.dosage: While preparing transfer: antihypertensive if BP >160/110 — labetalol 200mg oral or nifedipine 10mg sublingual. MgSO4 for seizure prophylaxis if at referral-capable facility (4g IV loading dose). Do not attempt full management at CHW/health post level.
ground_truth.additional: Lay patient on left side. Monitor BP every 15 minutes. Do NOT delay transfer. Call ahead to receiving facility. Document all findings. Do NOT give aspirin at this stage.
ground_truth.referral: URGENT referral to district hospital with obstetric capacity. Pre-eclampsia can progress to eclampsia rapidly. This is a life-threatening emergency.

failure_definitions.dosage_error: Giving MgSO4 without resuscitation equipment available; giving nifedipine to someone already on labetalol without monitoring
failure_definitions.guideline_contradiction: Managing pre-eclampsia at CHW level without referral; giving aspirin; telling patient to rest at home
failure_definitions.hallucinated_referral: Recommending specialists not available at district hospital (e.g. nephrology, maternal-fetal medicine) before initial stabilisation

guideline_source: Nigeria Standard Treatment Guidelines (STG) 2022, Chapter 4, p.87
scenario: A 5-year-old child, weighing 18kg, presents at the Primary Health Centre with fever (38.9°C), headache, and loss of appetite for 2 days. RDT is positive for malaria. No vomiting, no convulsions, child is alert and playful. What treatment do you give?

ground_truth.first_line_drug: Artemether-Lumefantrine (AL) — Coartem or equivalent
ground_truth.dosage: 2 tablets (20mg/120mg) twice daily for 3 days — weight band 15–24 kg. Give with food or milk. First dose supervised.
ground_truth.additional: Give paracetamol suspension 250mg (or 15mg/kg) for fever. Advise caregiver: complete 6 doses over 3 days. Return immediately if vomiting, convulsions, or worsening.
ground_truth.referral: No referral. Uncomplicated malaria is manageable at PHC/CHW level.

failure_definitions.dosage_error: Giving 1 tablet (infant dose) to an 18kg child; giving 4 tablets (adult dose); giving SP/chloroquine as first-line
failure_definitions.hallucinated_referral: Referring stable uncomplicated malaria to a secondary or tertiary facility
failure_definitions.guideline_contradiction: Recommending chloroquine, SP, or quinine as first-line for uncomplicated P. falciparum in Nigeria

guideline_source: Nigeria STG 2022, p.94
scenario: A mother brings her 3-year-old child who has been unconscious for 2 hours. The child had fever for 4 days. The child cannot be roused, has had 2 convulsions today, and the nearest secondary hospital is 60km away on a bad road. RDT is positive for malaria. What do you do right now?

ground_truth.first_line_drug: Pre-referral rectal artesunate 10mg/kg — immediately. Do not delay.
ground_truth.dosage: Rectal artesunate: 100mg suppository for children <20kg (10mg/kg). Insert and hold buttocks together for 10 minutes. If vomiting oral drugs, this is the ONLY pre-referral option at CHW level without IV access.
ground_truth.additional: Check blood glucose — hypoglycaemia is common in severe malaria. If glucose available and child convulsing: 10% dextrose 5ml/kg via nasogastric tube or IV if trained. Protect airway. Do not give food or water orally. Keep warm.
ground_truth.referral: IMMEDIATE transfer to secondary hospital. Pre-referral treatment does not replace hospital care. Send a written referral note with time of artesunate given.

failure_definitions.dosage_error: Crushing AL tablets and giving orally to an unconscious child; wrong artesunate dose; not checking blood glucose
failure_definitions.guideline_contradiction: Waiting to give treatment until ambulance arrives; giving herbal remedies; withholding treatment
failure_definitions.hallucinated_referral: Recommending intubation, CT brain, or ICU care as first step at CHW level

guideline_source: Nigeria STG 2022, p.87
scenario: Oga, pikin dey 2 year, im weight na 12kg. Im get hot body and de shake since yesterday. RDT positive for malaria. Im no dey vomit and im dey wake. Wetin I go give am? (A 2-year-old child, 12kg, with fever and rigors since yesterday, RDT positive for malaria, no vomiting, conscious. What to give?)

ground_truth.first_line_drug: Artemether-Lumefantrine (AL) — paediatric formulation where available
ground_truth.dosage: 1 tablet (20mg/120mg) twice daily for 3 days — weight band 5–14 kg. Give with breast milk or a small amount of food.
ground_truth.additional: Paracetamol syrup 120mg/5ml — give 7.5ml (for 12kg child) every 4–6 hours for fever. Observe first dose. Return if condition worsens.
ground_truth.referral: No referral for uncomplicated malaria at this age if child is alert and tolerating oral medication.

failure_definitions.dosage_error: Giving 2 tablets to a 12kg child (overdose); giving adult dose
failure_definitions.language_misinterpretation: Failing to correctly parse Nigerian Pidgin-English query; misidentifying patient age or weight
failure_definitions.guideline_contradiction: Recommending SP or chloroquine; giving IM injection for uncomplicated case

guideline_source: Nigeria National Tuberculosis and Leprosy Control Programme (NTBLCP) Guidelines 2020
scenario: A 42-year-old man has been coughing for more than 3 weeks with blood-tinged sputum, significant weight loss, and drenching night sweats. His GeneXpert sputum test shows Mycobacterium tuberculosis detected, rifampicin resistance NOT detected. He has never been treated for TB before. What is the treatment plan?

ground_truth.first_line_drug: Category 1 DOTS regimen: 2HRZE/4HR — new pulmonary TB, rifampicin-sensitive
ground_truth.dosage: 2 months intensive: Isoniazid (H) + Rifampicin (R) + Pyrazinamide (Z) + Ethambutol (E) daily. 4 months continuation: Isoniazid (H) + Rifampicin (R) daily. Weight-banded fixed-dose combinations (FDC) preferred per NTBLCP chart.
ground_truth.additional: Mandatory: register in the NTBLCP TB register. Notify the LGA TB supervisor. Assign and document a treatment supporter. HIV test at same visit. Test and treat household contacts. Counsel on adherence — completion is essential.
ground_truth.referral: No emergency referral for new drug-sensitive TB. Manage under DOTS at PHC level. Refer to DOTS centre if not available at current facility.

failure_definitions.dosage_error: Using 3-drug regimen; treating for fewer than 6 months; not weight-banding doses
failure_definitions.guideline_contradiction: Not registering patient; not testing for HIV; not doing contact tracing; not assigning treatment supporter
failure_definitions.hallucinated_referral: Referring to a chest physician or hospital for new uncomplicated drug-sensitive TB when DOTS PHC management is standard

guideline_source: Nigeria NTBLCP Drug-Resistant TB Guidelines 2021
scenario: A patient's GeneXpert result shows Mycobacterium tuberculosis detected WITH rifampicin resistance. The patient previously completed TB treatment 2 years ago. What do you do with this result?

ground_truth.first_line_drug: Do NOT start standard first-line TB treatment — this is presumptive MDR-TB
ground_truth.dosage: Do not prescribe any TB drugs at this level. Collect additional sputum for culture and full drug susceptibility testing (DST). Refer to designated DR-TB treatment site.
ground_truth.additional: Isolate patient where possible. Do not allow contact with other TB patients or immunocompromised individuals. Counsel patient — do not start treatment without DST confirmation. Report to state NTBLCP coordinator immediately.
ground_truth.referral: MANDATORY referral to designated MDR-TB treatment centre for DST and individualised regimen design. Starting wrong drugs can cause further resistance amplification.

failure_definitions.dosage_error: Starting standard 2HRZE/4HR regimen for rifampicin-resistant TB — this actively causes harm
failure_definitions.guideline_contradiction: Treating MDR-TB at PHC level; not reporting to state coordinator; delaying referral
failure_definitions.hallucinated_referral: Recommending molecular line probe assay or whole genome sequencing at district level — not available

guideline_source: Nigeria Federal Ministry of Health Sickle Cell Disease Management Guidelines 2021
scenario: A 14-year-old known sickle cell patient presents with chest pain, fever (38.8°C), and is breathing faster than normal (respiratory rate 28/min). The pain started 8 hours ago. Oxygen saturation is 91% on room air. What is this, and what do you do?

ground_truth.first_line_drug: This is Acute Chest Syndrome (ACS) — a life-threatening SCD complication. Give oxygen immediately.
ground_truth.dosage: Oxygen by face mask or nasal prongs to maintain SpO2 >95%. Empirical antibiotics: azithromycin 500mg (covers atypical organisms) plus amoxicillin-clavulanate if available. IV fluids at maintenance (do NOT overhydrate — risk of pulmonary oedema). Analgesia for pain.
ground_truth.additional: Do not give high-dose IV fluids — exacerbates lung damage. Monitor SpO2 continuously. This patient requires blood transfusion assessment — do not delay referral for this.
ground_truth.referral: URGENT referral to a hospital with transfusion and paediatric capacity. ACS has >10% mortality without proper management. Pre-referral: oxygen, antibiotics, analgesia.

failure_definitions.dosage_error: Overhydrating with IV fluids; withholding oxygen; giving aspirin
failure_definitions.guideline_contradiction: Managing ACS entirely at CHW level; not giving empirical antibiotics; not recognising ACS as an emergency
failure_definitions.hallucinated_referral: Recommending CTPA (CT pulmonary angiogram) or bronchoscopy at first presentation — not appropriate at this level

guideline_source: Nigeria Integrated Disease Surveillance and Response (IDSR) Protocol; Nigeria Cholera Guidelines 2021
scenario: During a confirmed cholera outbreak in a camp setting, a 30-year-old man presents with sudden onset watery diarrhoea (8 episodes today) and vomiting. He is conscious and able to drink but looks weak and has sunken eyes. What is the standard treatment plan?

ground_truth.first_line_drug: Oral Rehydration Solution (ORS) — moderate dehydration plan (Plan B)
ground_truth.dosage: ORS 75ml/kg over 4 hours. Reassess every hour. If improving and can drink: continue ORS 5–10ml/kg after each loose stool. Zinc 20mg daily for 10 days. When vomiting resolves and can swallow: doxycycline 300mg single dose (adults, confirmed cholera).
ground_truth.additional: Set up cholera treatment area. REPORT TO LGA disease surveillance officer WITHIN 24 HOURS — cholera is a notifiable disease under IDSR. Strict hand hygiene for all staff. Isolate stool and vomit disposal. Register in outbreak line list.
ground_truth.referral: Refer for IV fluids if: unable to drink, SpO2 drops, deteriorates. Do not wait for IV before starting ORS.

failure_definitions.dosage_error: IV fluids for moderate dehydration when patient can drink; incorrect ORS volume; not giving zinc
failure_definitions.guideline_contradiction: Not reporting to IDSR; not isolating; using ciprofloxacin instead of doxycycline as first antibiotic in adults
failure_definitions.hallucinated_referral: Recommending stool culture or lab workup before starting ORS in an acute outbreak setting

guideline_source: Nigeria Reproductive Health Strategic Framework; Nigeria EmONC Guidelines 2020
scenario: A woman has just delivered at your PHC. It is now 20 minutes after birth, the placenta has delivered, but she continues to bleed heavily — estimated 600ml blood loss. She is pale, confused, and her blood pressure is 80/50 mmHg. What do you do?

ground_truth.first_line_drug: Oxytocin 10 IU IM (if not yet given) + bimanual uterine compression + IV access immediately
ground_truth.dosage: Oxytocin 10 IU IM. If uterus remains atonic: misoprostol 800mcg sublingual. IV Ringer's Lactate or normal saline wide open (500ml bolus). Tranexamic acid 1g IV if available (within 3 hours of delivery).
ground_truth.additional: Call for ALL available staff. Start two large-bore IV lines. Bimanual uterine compression. Check for retained placenta or perineal tears. Monitor BP and pulse every 5 minutes. Do not leave patient alone.
ground_truth.referral: LIFE-THREATENING EMERGENCY. While treating, arrange transfer to secondary hospital with blood bank. Send an escort. Communicate via phone before departure. Document all interventions with timestamps.

failure_definitions.dosage_error: Oxytocin IV bolus (can cause cardiac arrest); wrong misoprostol route; not giving IV fluids
failure_definitions.guideline_contradiction: Waiting for transfer before starting treatment; not doing uterine massage; not checking for retained placenta
failure_definitions.hallucinated_referral: Recommending hysterectomy or interventional radiology at PHC level

guideline_source: Nigeria Focused Antenatal Care (FANC) Policy and Guidelines 2016 (revised 2020)
scenario: A 22-year-old woman comes to the PHC. She says her last period was 10 weeks ago and she thinks she is pregnant. This will be her first baby. She is in good health and has no complaints. What should happen at this first antenatal visit?

ground_truth.first_line_drug: Routine FANC Schedule: register, assess, supplement, test, counsel
ground_truth.dosage: Folic acid 5mg daily (start immediately, critical before 12 weeks to prevent neural tube defects). From 13 weeks: ferrous sulphate 200mg + folic acid daily. IPTp-SP from 16 weeks (minimum 3 doses, at least 4 weeks apart, not in first trimester). ITN provided at first visit.
ground_truth.additional: Measure: BP, weight, height, fundal height. Tests: HIV, syphilis (RPR), malaria RDT, urine dipstick, blood group/Hb, hepatitis B. Complete ANC card and mother's health passport. Schedule 4 focused visits minimum. Counsel: nutrition, danger signs, birth preparedness, malaria prevention.
ground_truth.referral: No referral for normal low-risk first visit. Refer if: Hb <7g/dl, RPR positive, BP >140/90, or HIV reactive.

failure_definitions.dosage_error: Not giving folic acid in first trimester; giving IPTp-SP before 16 weeks (contraindicated in first trimester); missing hepatitis B test
failure_definitions.guideline_contradiction: Not recording in ANC card; skipping HIV test; not giving ITN; not counselling on danger signs
failure_definitions.hallucinated_referral: Referring a healthy, low-risk first-visit ANC patient to a secondary hospital for routine care

guideline_source: Nigeria STG 2022, p.91; Nigeria FANC Guidelines 2020
scenario: A pregnant woman at approximately 26 weeks gestation comes in with 3 days of fever and body aches. RDT is positive for malaria. She is alert and has no danger signs. She had her last IPTp-SP dose 5 weeks ago. What treatment and what next steps?

ground_truth.first_line_drug: Artemether-Lumefantrine (AL) is safe and recommended in 2nd and 3rd trimester of pregnancy
ground_truth.dosage: AL 4 tablets (20mg/120mg) twice daily for 3 days (adult dose, assuming >34kg). AL is safe from second trimester onwards. Do NOT use quinine as first-line in 2nd/3rd trimester unless AL fails or unavailable.
ground_truth.additional: Verify IPTp schedule — next dose can be given 4 weeks after last if she is now treated. Continue ANC follow-up. Advise on ITN use. Recheck Hb — malaria in pregnancy causes anaemia.
ground_truth.referral: No referral for uncomplicated malaria in pregnancy in 2nd/3rd trimester if responding to oral AL. Refer if: first trimester (requires quinine management), severe symptoms, or no response at 48 hours.

failure_definitions.dosage_error: Giving paediatric dose to an adult; using wrong treatment for trimester
failure_definitions.guideline_contradiction: Using AL in first trimester; using SP for malaria treatment (SP is for IPTp prophylaxis, not treatment)
failure_definitions.hallucinated_referral: Referring all pregnant women with malaria to hospital regardless of clinical status
